DiagramRecommendation.description: A single recommendation for a diagram to add to a report section.

diagram_type.description: One of the supported DIAGRAM_TYPES keys
diagram_type.title: Diagram Type

description.description: What the diagram should show, grounded in the section content
description.title: Description

DiagramRecommendation.title: DiagramRecommendation

schema_json.description: Full analysis output: which sections deserve diagrams.

recommendations.description: 2-5 sections that would benefit from a visual diagram
recommendations.title: Recommendations

schema_json.title: DiagramAnalysisResult

system:
You are a research report visualization specialist. You identify which report sections benefit most from visual diagrams and select the appropriate diagram type for each.

Available diagram types:
- "process_flow": How X works (step-by-step process)
- "comparison_matrix": X vs Y vs Z comparison
- "causal_chain": A causes B which leads to C
- "hierarchy": Classification/taxonomy of findings
- "timeline": Chronological development of topic
- "pros_cons": Advantages vs disadvantages
- "decision_tree": If X then Y, else Z

Output format example:
{"recommendations": [{"section_id": "s01", "section_title": "Mechanisms of Action", "diagram_type": "process_flow", "description": "Step-by-step process of how compound X binds to receptor Y, triggers pathway Z, and produces effect W as described in the section"}]}
prompt:
Given these report sections, identify 2-5 that would benefit from a visual diagram. For each, specify the diagram type and what the diagram should show.

SECTIONS:
--- Section s01: Overview and Definitions (394 words) ---
Intermittent fasting refers to a family of dietary patterns that alternate defined periods of energy restriction or abstention with periods of unrestricted or ad libitum eating, rather than prescribing specific foods or macronutrient targets. Contemporary research and clinical practice distinguish several principal protocols. Time-restricted eating compresses daily caloric intake into a fixed window, typically ranging from four to twelve hours, with the remaining hours of the day reserved for water, non-caloric beverages, or complete abstention; common variants include 16:8 and 14:10 schedules. The 5:2 approach, sometimes described as modified fasting, permits habitual eating on five days of the week and restricts intake to roughly 500 to 600 kilocalories on two non-consecutive days. Alternate-day fasting cycles between a fast day, on which intake is either eliminated or reduced to a small fraction of energy needs, and a feed day without explicit restriction. Periodic or prolonged fasting, encompassing regimens of twenty-four hours or longer, and the fasting-mimicking diet represent additional variants that extend the duration of restriction beyond a single day.

These protocols share a common physiological premise: a sustained shift away from continuous postprandial metabolism toward periods of negative energy balance, glycogen depletion, and altered hormonal signaling. They differ, however, in the duration, frequency, and severity of the fasting interval, and consequently i [...]

--- Section s02: Key Findings and Benefits (462 words) ---
With the dietary patterns and terminology of intermittent fasting now characterized, attention turns to the primary positive outcomes that clinical and translational research has most consistently associated with these regimens. Reported benefits span metabolic, cardiovascular, and cellular domains, though the strength and generalizability of the evidence vary considerably across outcome categories and study designs.

Weight and adiposity reduction are the most commonly documented outcomes. Trials of time-restricted eating, alternate-day fasting, and the 5:2 approach have generally produced modest reductions in body weight relative to baseline, with effects that are in many cases comparable to, rather than clearly superior to, conventional continuous calorie restriction. Reductions in waist circumference and fat mass have also been described in some studies, suggesting that improvements are not limited to scale weight. However, the magnitude of loss tends to plateau over time, and attrition in longer trials limits certainty about durability beyond six to twelve months.

Glycemic outcomes represent a second area of frequent interest. In adults with overweight, obesity, prediabetes, or type 2 diabetes, intermittent fasting protocols have been reported to improve fasting glucose, insulin sensitivity, and in some studies glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c), though effect sizes are typically modest and not uniformly observed. Improvements appear more consistent when fasting regimens produ [...]

--- Section s03: Mechanisms of Action (541 words) ---
Having catalogued the clinical outcomes most consistently associated with intermittent fasting, the next question is how such outcomes arise at the molecular, cellular, and systemic levels. The mechanistic account outlined below integrates the pathways most frequently invoked in the literature, while acknowledging that much of the underlying evidence derives from rodent and in vitro work and has not been uniformly confirmed in humans.

A central trigger appears to be the metabolic switch that occurs when hepatic glycogen stores become depleted, typically after twelve to sixteen hours without caloric intake. As circulating glucose and insulin decline, adipose tissue lipolysis accelerates and free fatty acids are shuttled to the liver, where beta-oxidation yields ketone bodies such as beta-hydroxybutyrate. Beta-hydroxybutyrate is not merely a fuel substrate: it also functions as a signaling molecule that inhibits class I histone deacetylases and modulates the expression of genes involved in oxidative stress resistance, mitochondrial biogenesis, and inflammatory tone. This shift from glucose to fatty acid and ketone utilization is the proposed substrate for many downstream effects.

Falling cellular energy charge activates AMP-activated protein kinase, a conserved energy sensor that promotes catabolic, ATP-generating pathways while suppressing anabolic processes. In parallel, reduced amino acid and growth factor signaling dampens the mechanistic target of rapamycin complex 1, a  [...]

--- Section s05: Safety Profile and Risks (494 words) ---
Having traced the molecular and systemic pathways through which intermittent fasting exerts its effects, the next question is how those same pathways translate into adverse events, contraindications, and population-specific risks. A mechanism that drives therapeutic benefit in one person may produce harm in another, so a balanced appraisal of the evidence requires explicit attention to the safety profile that accompanies any period of deliberate energy restriction.

The most commonly reported adverse effects of intermittent fasting are transient and symptomatic rather than organ-specific. Headache, fatigue, dizziness, difficulty concentrating, irritability, low mood, constipation, bad breath, and pronounced hunger cluster in the first one to four weeks of adaptation and typically attenuate as metabolic flexibility improves. Sleep disturbance and cold intolerance are also described, and a minority of adherents report transient orthostatic symptoms attributable to shifts in fluid and electrolyte balance. Binge-like rebound eating during non-fasting windows represents a behavioral adverse effect that can erode both the metabolic and psychological intent of the regimen. Uncommon but more serious concerns include symptomatic hypoglycemia, dehydration, electrolyte disturbances, gallstone formation during rapid weight loss, and, in vulnerable individuals, the unmasking of disordered eating patterns.

Several groups should be considered at absolute or near-absolute contraindication.  [...]

--- Section s06: Special Populations (493 words) ---
Having mapped the safety profile and general contraindications of intermittent fasting, attention now shifts to how those same benefits and hazards translate when the intervention is deployed in demographically or clinically distinct groups, where baseline physiology, comorbidity burden, and competing nutritional demands can materially alter the risk-benefit calculus.

In adults with type 2 diabetes, intermittent fasting has been explored as an adjunct to standard pharmacotherapy, with the most frequently cited outcomes involving modest reductions in glycated hemoglobin, fasting glucose, and body weight, along with opportunities for antihyperglycemic medication de-escalation. The central clinical concern is iatrogenic hypoglycemia, particularly among patients on sulfonylureas or insulin, which makes individualized medication adjustment, glucose self-monitoring, and clinician supervision prerequisites rather than optional refinements. Women with polycystic ovary syndrome represent a related metabolic population in whom time-restricted and alternate-day protocols have been examined for their capacity to improve insulin sensitivity, androgen profiles, menstrual regularity, and anthropometric indices; available trials are small, heterogeneous in protocol, and short in duration, so conclusions about reproductive and long-term metabolic outcomes remain preliminary. In metabolic syndrome more broadly, intermittent fasting has been studied as a lifestyle strategy targeting the cluste [...]

--- Section s08: Clinical Implications (536 words) ---
Having mapped how intermittent fasting behaves across pregnant, pediatric, geriatric, and chronically ill subgroups, the final question is how clinicians should operationalize this body of evidence in day-to-day practice. The translation from trial data to the examination room is complicated by the breadth of protocols, the heterogeneity of reported outcomes, and the absence of head-to-head comparisons against established dietary standards, so any recommendation must be framed as provisional rather than definitive.

Intermittent fasting may reasonably be considered as one option, not a first-line prescription, for adults with metabolic syndrome, insulin resistance, or obesity who have struggled to sustain continuous caloric restriction and who express a clear preference for time-structured eating. Candidate protocols include 16:8 time-restricted eating, 5:2 modified fasting, and alternate-day approaches, with selection driven by the patient's daily schedule, occupational demands, social context, and willingness to monitor intake. Clinicians should frame intermittent fasting as a behavioral tool that achieves energy deficit through timing rather than a metabolically unique intervention, since the bulk of comparative data suggest that weight loss outcomes converge with those of conventional calorie counting when adherence is matched.

Contraindications warrant explicit documentation before initiation. Absolute or near-absolute contraindications include pregnancy, lactation, a c [...]

Constraints:
- Select at most 5 sections.
- Only recommend a diagram if it adds genuine clarity beyond the text.
- The description must reference specific facts or structures present in the section content.
- Choose the diagram type that best fits the section's analytical structure.
